content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

Warcraft III UI Component Library — a showcase/demo site that recreates WC3's in-game and menu UI elements as web components. Built with **SolidJS** + **TypeScript** + **Vite**. The app renders race-themed (Human, Orc, NightElf, Undead) UI elements including HUD bars, buttons, panels, tooltips, borders, and 3D model portraits.

## Commands

- `npm run dev` — start dev server on port 3000
- `npm run build` — production build (target: esnext)
- `npm run preview` — preview production build

No test runner or linter is configured.

## Architecture

### Rendering Pipeline

The app uses a **dual rendering approach**:

1. **CSS/DOM rendering** — Standard SolidJS components for most UI (buttons, bars, tooltips, panels). Styling is primarily in `src/global.css` (~45KB) using CSS custom properties for race theming.
2. **WebGL overlay** — A full-viewport `<canvas>` composited over the DOM for elements that need GPU rendering:
   - `src/renderer/` — Custom WebGL2 rendering engine with texture atlas support, nine-slice border drawing, quad batching, and blend mode management.
   - `src/components/TopHudWebGL.tsx` — Separate WebGL1 canvas for the top console HUD tiles.
   - 3D model components (`HeroPortraitModel`, `WorkerUnitModel`, `TimeIndicatorModel`) use the `war3-model` library to render MDX models.

The main `Renderer` (`src/renderer/Renderer.ts`) operates as an element registry pattern: DOM components register themselves (checkboxes, sliders, radio buttons, edit boxes, glue buttons, menu frames) and the renderer draws WebGL decorations positioned relative to those DOM elements each frame.

### State Management

Simple SolidJS signals in `src/state/`:
- `race.ts` — Current race selection (drives all race-themed visuals)
- `resources.ts` — Gold/lumber counters with animated interpolation and WC3 cheat codes (greedisgood, keysersoze, leafittome)
- `ui.ts` — Animated bar percentages and loading bar state

### Texture Handling

WC3 assets are served from `public/` in BLP format (Blizzard's texture format). The `war3-model` library's `decodeBLP`/`getBLPImageData` functions convert BLP textures to ImageData for WebGL upload. PNG textures are used for some elements (console tiles, tooltips).

### Key Types

`src/lib/types.ts` defines `Race` (`'Human' | 'Orc' | 'NightElf' | 'Undead'`) and `RacePrefix` which maps each race to its various naming conventions used in asset paths.

### Component Organization

- `src/components/` — Reusable UI primitives (StatBar, Checkbox, Slider, RadioButton, EditBox, MenuButton, MenuPanel, Tooltip, LoadingBar, etc.)
- `src/components/sections/` — Page sections that compose primitives into the showcase layout
- `src/App.tsx` — Main app shell; orchestrates race switching, resource animation ticks, WebGL renderer lifecycle, and section layout

toolUseResult.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

Warcraft III UI Component Library — a showcase/demo site that recreates WC3's in-game and menu UI elements as web components. Built with **SolidJS** + **TypeScript** + **Vite**. The app renders race-themed (Human, Orc, NightElf, Undead) UI elements including HUD bars, buttons, panels, tooltips, borders, and 3D model portraits.

## Commands

- `npm run dev` — start dev server on port 3000
- `npm run build` — production build (target: esnext)
- `npm run preview` — preview production build

No test runner or linter is configured.

## Architecture

### Rendering Pipeline

The app uses a **dual rendering approach**:

1. **CSS/DOM rendering** — Standard SolidJS components for most UI (buttons, bars, tooltips, panels). Styling is primarily in `src/global.css` (~45KB) using CSS custom properties for race theming.
2. **WebGL overlay** — A full-viewport `<canvas>` composited over the DOM for elements that need GPU rendering:
   - `src/renderer/` — Custom WebGL2 rendering engine with texture atlas support, nine-slice border drawing, quad batching, and blend mode management.
   - `src/components/TopHudWebGL.tsx` — Separate WebGL1 canvas for the top console HUD tiles.
   - 3D model components (`HeroPortraitModel`, `WorkerUnitModel`, `TimeIndicatorModel`) use the `war3-model` library to render MDX models.

The main `Renderer` (`src/renderer/Renderer.ts`) operates as an element registry pattern: DOM components register themselves (checkboxes, sliders, radio buttons, edit boxes, glue buttons, menu frames) and the renderer draws WebGL decorations positioned relative to those DOM elements each frame.

### State Management

Simple SolidJS signals in `src/state/`:
- `race.ts` — Current race selection (drives all race-themed visuals)
- `resources.ts` — Gold/lumber counters with animated interpolation and WC3 cheat codes (greedisgood, keysersoze, leafittome)
- `ui.ts` — Animated bar percentages and loading bar state

### Texture Handling

WC3 assets are served from `public/` in BLP format (Blizzard's texture format). The `war3-model` library's `decodeBLP`/`getBLPImageData` functions convert BLP textures to ImageData for WebGL upload. PNG textures are used for some elements (console tiles, tooltips).

### Key Types

`src/lib/types.ts` defines `Race` (`'Human' | 'Orc' | 'NightElf' | 'Undead'`) and `RacePrefix` which maps each race to its various naming conventions used in asset paths.

### Component Organization

- `src/components/` — Reusable UI primitives (StatBar, Checkbox, Slider, RadioButton, EditBox, MenuButton, MenuPanel, Tooltip, LoadingBar, etc.)
- `src/components/sections/` — Page sections that compose primitives into the showcase layout
- `src/App.tsx` — Main app shell; orchestrates race switching, resource animation ticks, WebGL renderer lifecycle, and section layout

## SolidJS Migration Scope Analysis: /home/garrosh/repos/wcui

Based on a thorough analysis of all `.tsx` and `.ts` source files, here's a complete catalog of SolidJS-specific patterns used in this project:

---

### **State Management Files (src/state/)**

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/ui.ts**
- **Signals**: `createSignal()` used 4 times
  - `[healthPct, setHealthPct]` = 75%
  - `[manaPct, setManaPct]` = 60%
  - `[xpPct, setXpPct]` = 40%
  - `[buildPct, setBuildPct]` = 55%
  - `[progress, setProgress]` for loading bar (0 initial)
- **Pattern**: Custom state factories (`createAnimatedBars()`, `createLoadingBar()`) returning signal getters/setters
- **Lifecycle**: `setInterval` managed manually with cleanup in `stop()` function

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/resources.ts**
- **Signals**: `createSignal()` used 4 times (module-level)
  - `[goldTarget, setGoldTarget]` = 500
  - `[goldCurrent, setGoldCurrent]` = 500
  - `[lumberTarget, setLumberTarget]` = 300
  - `[lumberCurrent, setLumberCurrent]` = 300
- **Pattern**: Global module-level signals (not wrapped in functions)
- **Reactive Functions**: `tickResources()`, `animateCounters()` use closure over signals
- **Imperative State Updates**: `setGoldTarget(prev => ...)` pattern used extensively

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/race.ts**
- **Signal**: 1x `createSignal<Race>('Human')` module-level
  - `[currentRace, setCurrentRace]`
- **Pattern**: Global race selection state
- **Type Usage**: `Accessor<Race>` type exported implicitly through signal accessors

---

### **Entry Point**

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/index.tsx**
- **SolidJS Imports**: `{ render } from 'solid-js/web'`
- **Pattern**: `render(() => <App />, root)` - Standard SolidJS app initialization

---

### **Main App Component**

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/App.tsx** (Large component ~1300 lines)
- **Imports**: `{ createEffect, createSignal, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signals Created**: 100+ signal pairs for texture URLs, state management
  - `[hovered, setHovered]`, `[pressed, setPressed]` (UI interactions)
  - `[upperButtonAtlasUrl, setUpperButtonAtlasUrl]` and 60+ similar texture URL signals
  - Pattern: Each image/texture resource gets its own signal
- **Lifecycle Hooks**:
  - `onMount()`: Texture loading, renderer initialization
  - `createEffect()`: Reactive data updates
  - `onCleanup()`: Resource cleanup
- **Reactive Patterns**:
  - Derived accessors: `() => goldCurrent()` pattern for deriving UI state
  - Conditional rendering via ternary operators with signal calls

---

### **Component Files - Lifecycle & Refs**

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/TopHud.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ createSignal, onMount, onCleanup, For } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signals**: `[hovered, setHovered]`, `[pressed, setPressed]`
- **Lifecycle**: `onMount()` to register DOM refs with renderer
- **Control Flow**: `<For each={RACES}>{(race) => ...}</For>` - SolidJS list rendering

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/Checkbox.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ createSignal, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signal**: `[checked, setChecked]` with initial value from props
- **Ref**: Template ref `ref={ref}` registered in `onMount()`
- **DOM Sync**: Manual ref passing to renderer object

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/RadioButton.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount } from 'solid-js'` + `type { Accessor } from 'solid-js'`
- **Props Pattern**: Accepts `selected: Accessor<boolean>` (signal as prop)
- **Reactive Binding**: `classList={{ selected: props.selected() }}` - accessor invocation in JSX
- **Lifecycle**: `onMount()` for DOM registration

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/Slider.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ createSignal, onMount, onCleanup } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signal**: `[value, setValue]` initialized from props
- **Cleanup**: `onCleanup(onUp)` - Manual event listener cleanup
- **Manual State**: Local `let dragging` variable (non-reactive)

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/HeroPortraitModel.tsx** (314 lines, complex WebGL)
- **Imports**: `{ createEffect, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signals**: `[status, setStatus]`, `[widgetMaskUrl, setWidgetMaskUrl]`
- **Lifecycle Hooks**:
  - `onMount()`: WebGL context setup, observer initialization, render loop start
  - `createEffect()`: React to prop changes (race), trigger model reloading
  - `onCleanup()`: Cleanup RAF, observers, renderer destruction
- **Pattern**: Version tracking + cleanup guard (`disposed`, `initVersion`) for async safety
- **Async Handling**: Promise-based asset loading with cancellation guards

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/WorkerUnitModel.tsx** (416 lines, complex WebGL)
- **Imports**: `{ createEffect, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Lifecycle**: Identical to HeroPortraitModel - onMount, createEffect, onCleanup
- **Async Pattern**: Version-based cancellation for long-running async operations

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/TimeIndicatorModel.tsx** (408 lines, WebGL model)
- **Imports**: `{ createEffect, createSignal, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Signals**: `[status, setStatus]`, `[widgetMaskUrl, setWidgetMaskUrl]`
- **Lifecycle Pattern**: Same as other WebGL components
- **Advanced Cleanup**: `onCleanup()` handles complex teardown of GL resources

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/TopHudWebGL.tsx** (405 lines, WebGL rendering)
- **Imports**: `{ createEffect, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Lifecycle Hooks**:
  - `onMount()`: WebGL setup, event listeners, ResizeObserver
  - `createEffect()` (2x): React to prop changes (race, leftOnly)
  - `onCleanup()`: Comprehensive resource cleanup inside `onCleanup()` callback
- **Complex Cleanup**: Nested `onCleanup()` inside `onMount()` for granular control

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/CursorOverlay.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount, onCleanup } from 'solid-js'`
- **Reactive Accessor**: `const bgImage = () => ...` - function as accessor
- **Manual Events**: Direct DOM event listener management

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/EditBox.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Ref Pattern**: Template ref registration with renderer

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/StatBar.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount, type Accessor } from 'solid-js'`
- **Props**: Accepts `fillPercent: Accessor<number>` (signal passed from parent)
- **Accessor Usage**: `props.fillPercent()` called in render

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/LoadingBar.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount, type Accessor } from 'solid-js'`
- **Props**: `progress: Accessor<number>` from parent signal
- **Render**: Calls accessor to get values

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/MenuButton.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount, type JSX } from 'solid-js'`
- **Pattern**: Simple template ref registration

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/MenuPanel.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- **Ref**: Simple registration pattern

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/Tooltip.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ onMount, type JSX } from 'solid-js'`
- **Ref**: Registration with renderer

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/BottomHud.tsx**
- **Imports**: None (pure UI)
- **Pattern**: Functional with accessor functions for derived values
  - `const tileSrc = (n: string) => ...`

#### **/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/HeroGallery.tsx**
- **Imports**: `{ For } from 'solid-js'`
- **Control Flow**: `<For each={raceHeroes()}>{(hero) => ...}</For>`
- **Derived Accessor**: `const raceHeroes = () => HEROES[currentRace()]`

Here's the full scope. The migration touches ~30 source files. I propose breaking it into these phases:

**Phase 1 — Infrastructure**: Swap dependencies (solid-js → react/react-dom), update vite config, tsconfig, and entry point.

**Phase 2 — State layer** (`src/state/`): Convert module-level signals to a React-compatible pattern. Since SolidJS signals work outside components, we'll use a lightweight pub-sub store (or Zustand) so the existing import-and-call pattern survives without wrapping everything in Context.

**Phase 3 — Leaf components**: Convert simple components (StatBar, Checkbox, RadioButton, Slider, EditBox, MenuButton, MenuPanel, Tooltip, LoadingBar, BottomHud, CursorOverlay, HeroGallery).

**Phase 4 — Section components**: Convert all `sections/` files — replace `<For>` with `.map()`, `<Show>` with conditional expressions, `Accessor<T>` with plain `T` props.

**Phase 5 — WebGL components**: Convert the complex lifecycle components (TopHudWebGL, HeroPortraitModel, WorkerUnitModel, TimeIndicatorModel) — these need careful `useEffect` cleanup.

**Phase 6 — App.tsx**: The big one (~1300 lines, 100+ signals). Convert last since it depends on everything else.

Key mapping: `createSignal` → `useState`, `createEffect` → `useEffect`, `onMount` → `useEffect(..., [])`, `onCleanup` → effect cleanup returns, `<For>` → `.map()`, `<Show>` → `&&`/ternary, `Accessor<T>` → plain `T`.

Phase 1 is done. Here's what changed:

**Dependencies**:
- Removed: `solid-js`, `vite-plugin-solid`
- Added: `react` 19.2, `react-dom` 19.2, `@vitejs/plugin-react` 4.7, `@types/react`, `@types/react-dom`

**Config**:
- `vite.config.ts` — switched from `vite-plugin-solid` to `@vitejs/plugin-react`
- `tsconfig.json` — `jsx: "react-jsx"`, `jsxImportSource: "react"`

**Entry point**:
- `src/index.tsx` — switched from `render()` (solid-js/web) to `createRoot().render()` (react-dom/client)

The rest of the source files still have SolidJS imports so they won't compile yet — that's expected. Ready for Phase 2 (state layer)?
Let me re-read all three state files to make sure I have the full content.

The key challenge: SolidJS signals work at module level, but React's `useState` only works inside components. I'll use `useSyncExternalStore` for the global stores (race, resources) and convert the factory functions in ui.ts to React hooks.

First, a small utility, then converting each state file.

content.text:
No errors in the state files. Phase 2 is done.

**What changed:**

- **New `src/state/createStore.ts`** — Tiny external store utility using `useSyncExternalStore`. Provides `get()` (read anywhere), `set()` (write anywhere), and `useValue()` (React hook for components).

- **`src/state/race.ts`** — Module-level signal replaced with `createExternalStore<Race>('Human')`. Exports `currentRace` (getter), `setCurrentRace` (setter), and new `useCurrentRace` (hook).

- **`src/state/resources.ts`** — Four module-level signals replaced with four external stores. Same exported functions (`tickResources`, `animateCounters`, `handleCheatKey`) plus new hooks (`useGoldCurrent`, `useLumberCurrent`, etc.) for React components.

- **`src/state/ui.ts`** — `createAnimatedBars()` → `useAnimatedBars()`, `createLoadingBar()` → `useLoadingBar()`. Now React hooks using `useState`/`useRef`/`useCallback`. Return values are now plain numbers instead of accessor functions.

The API change to watch in later phases: values that were `Accessor<T>` (called as `foo()`) will become plain `T` (just `foo`). Ready for Phase 3?

content.text:
Both errors are pre-existing — `HEROES` and `HeroData` don't exist in the source (they were already missing before the migration). No errors introduced by our conversion.

Phase 3 is done. Here's the summary of changes across all 12 leaf components:

**Pattern conversions applied:**
- `import { onMount, onCleanup, createSignal, For } from 'solid-js'` → `import { useEffect, useRef, useState } from 'react'`
- `let ref!: HTMLDivElement` → `useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null)` + `.current` access
- `onMount(() => {...})` → `useEffect(() => {...}, [])`
- `onCleanup(() => {...})` → `useEffect` return cleanup
- `createSignal(x)` → `useState(x)`
- `Accessor<T>` props → plain `T` props (StatBar, LoadingBar, RadioButton)
- `class=` → `className=`
- `classList={{ checked: checked() }}` → template string className
- `<For each={...}>{(item) => ...}</For>` → `.map()` with `key` props
- `JSX.Element` → `ReactNode`
- `style={{ 'font-size': ... }}` → `style={{ fontSize: ... }}` (camelCase)
- `currentRace()` calls → `useCurrentRace()` hook

Ready for Phase 4 (section components)?

lines: Beveled, gold-trimmed buttons with faction-aware theming and glow states. Your design system has a primary button. Ours has four.

Animated numeric display with icon slot. For when a number needs to feel important. Ticks up smoothly like gold flowing into your treasury.
A spatial action grid with hotkey hints, cooldown states, and empty-slot affordances. Faster than a command palette — your users already know the layout.
A task queue with progress indicator and cancelable pending slots. Perfect for managing build pipelines, AI agent workflows, or anything that trains in 30 seconds.
Rich content tooltips with title, description, hotkey badge, and inline resource costs. Your title attribute could never.
Basic frontline infantry. Strong against early pressure and pairs with priest support.
A hero-tier avatar frame with live 3D portrait rendering. Because your users deserve better than a circle crop.
Health, mana, XP, build, and fullscreen loading bars with animated drain, fill, and layered overlays.

content.text:
All remaining errors are in `App.tsx` (Phase 6) and the pre-existing missing modules (`GlueButton`, `OptionButton`, `HEROES`, `HeroData`). The WebGL components (Phase 5) and the renderer (not being converted — it's pure WebGL, not SolidJS) are clean.

Phase 4 is done. **14 section files converted:**

- `ShowcaseSection.tsx` — `<For>` → `.map()`, `classList` → className string, `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()`, `JSX.Element` → `ReactNode`, `createEffect`/`onCleanup` → `useEffect` with cleanup
- `BordersSection.tsx` — `<Show>` → ternary, nested `<For>` → `.map()`, `createSignal` → `useState`, `onMount` → `useEffect`
- `FactionPreviewSection.tsx` — Complex component with multiple effects, `Accessor<T>` props → plain `T`, `onInput` → `onChange`
- `ControlsSection.tsx` — `createSignal` → `useState`, `Accessor<boolean>` callback props → plain `boolean`
- `ButtonsSection.tsx` — `<For>` → `.map()`, accessor function items → plain strings, `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()`
- `BarsSection.tsx` — `Accessor<number>` props → plain `number`
- `HeroSection.tsx` — Canvas ref callback → `RefObject`, `class` → `className`
- `DesignTokensSection.tsx` — `createEffect`/`onMount`/`onCleanup` → `useEffect`, `let ref!` → `useRef`
- `BundleSizeSection.tsx` — Same pattern, `Accessor<string>` → plain `string`
- `AccessibilitySection.tsx`, `FAQSection.tsx` — Just `class` → `className`
- `FooterSection.tsx` — CSS custom property style cast
- `IconsSection.tsx` — Removed unused `currentRace` import, `class` → `className`
- `PanelsSection.tsx` — `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()`

Ready for Phase 5 (WebGL components)?
Now I'll convert all 5 WebGL components. The key challenge: SolidJS components run once, so `let` variables persist. In React, I need `useRef` for all mutable WebGL state.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user requested converting the entire project from SolidJS to React, while keeping the same behavior. They explicitly wanted to do it in parts to ensure everything keeps working at each step. A 6-phase migration plan was agreed upon:
   - Phase 1: Infrastructure (deps, config, entry point)
   - Phase 2: State layer (src/state/ files)
   - Phase 3: Leaf components (12 simple components)
   - Phase 4: Section components (14 section components)
   - Phase 5: WebGL components (5 complex components)
   - Phase 6: App.tsx (the main ~1300+ line component with 100+ signals)

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - SolidJS → React migration patterns:
     - `createSignal` → `useState` (local) or `createExternalStore` with `useSyncExternalStore` (global/module-level)
     - `createEffect` → `useEffect` with dependency arrays
     - `onMount` → `useEffect(() => {...}, [])`
     - `onCleanup` → return cleanup function from `useEffect`
     - `<For each={...}>` → `.map()` with `key` props
     - `<Show when={...}>` → ternary/`&&` conditional
     - `Accessor<T>` → plain `T` (values instead of getter functions)
     - `classList={{...}}` → template string className
     - `class=` → `className=`
     - `JSX.Element` → `ReactNode`
     - SolidJS kebab-case styles → React camelCase styles
     - `let ref!: HTMLElement` → `useRef<HTMLElement>(null)` + `.current`
   - For WebGL components: SolidJS runs component function once (mutable `let` vars persist), React re-runs on every render → all persistent mutable state stored in `useRef` objects
   - `useSyncExternalStore` for module-level global state that can be read/written from anywhere
   - Props ref pattern (`propsRef.current = props`) to access latest props from stale closures in WebGL render loops

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **Phase 1 - Infrastructure:**
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/package.json`
     - Swapped dependencies: removed solid-js + vite-plugin-solid, added react 19.2 + react-dom 19.2 + @vitejs/plugin-react 4.7 + @types/react + @types/react-dom
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/vite.config.ts`
     - Changed from solidPlugin to react plugin
     ```ts
     import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
     import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
     export default defineConfig({
       plugins: [react()],
       server: { port: 3000 },
       build: { target: 'esnext' },
     });
     ```
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/tsconfig.json`
     - Changed `"jsx": "preserve"` → `"jsx": "react-jsx"`, `"jsxImportSource": "solid-js"` → `"jsxImportSource": "react"`
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/index.tsx`
     - Entry point converted from SolidJS render to React createRoot
     ```tsx
     import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
     import './global.css';
     import './showcase-tooltip-center.css';
     import App from './App';
     const root = document.getElementById('root');
     if (!root) throw new Error('Root element not found');
     createRoot(root).render(<App />);
     ```

   **Phase 2 - State Layer:**
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/createStore.ts` (NEW)
     - External store utility using `useSyncExternalStore` for module-level state
     ```ts
     import { useSyncExternalStore } from 'react';
     export function createExternalStore<T>(initialValue: T) {
       let value = initialValue;
       const listeners = new Set<() => void>();
       function get(): T { return value; }
       function set(next: T | ((prev: T) => T)): void {
         const nextValue = typeof next === 'function' ? (next as (prev: T) => T)(value) : next;
         if (!Object.is(nextValue, value)) { value = nextValue; listeners.forEach(l => l()); }
       }
       function subscribe(listener: () => void): () => void {
         listeners.add(listener); return () => { listeners.delete(listener); };
       }
       function useValue(): T { return useSyncExternalStore(subscribe, get); }
       return { get, set, useValue };
     }
     ```
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/race.ts`
     - Module-level signal → `createExternalStore<Race>('Human')`
     - Exports: `currentRace` (getter fn), `setCurrentRace` (setter), `useCurrentRace` (hook)
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/resources.ts`
     - 4 module-level signals → 4 external stores
     - Exports getters (`goldCurrent`, `lumberCurrent`, etc.), setters, hooks (`useGoldCurrent`, etc.)
     - `tickResources`, `animateCounters`, `handleCheatKey` work the same using store `.get()` and `.set()`
   
   - `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/ui.ts`
     - `createAnimatedBars()` → `useAnimatedBars()` (React hook with useState + useCallback)
     - `createLoadingBar()` → `useLoadingBar()` (React hook with useState + useRef + useCallback, uses startRef for recursive setTimeout)
     - Return values changed from accessor functions to plain values

   **Phase 3 - Leaf Components (12 files):**
   All converted with standard patterns. Key files:
   
   - `src/components/StatBar.tsx` - `Accessor<number>` → plain `number` prop
   - `src/components/Checkbox.tsx` - `createSignal` → `useState`, `classList` → className string
   - `src/components/RadioButton.tsx` - `Accessor<boolean>` → plain `boolean` prop
   - `src/components/Slider.tsx` - Complex: drag handling moved to useEffect with native event listeners, `let dragging` → `useRef(false)`
   - `src/components/EditBox.tsx`, `MenuButton.tsx`, `MenuPanel.tsx`, `Tooltip.tsx` - Simple ref registration pattern
   - `src/components/LoadingBar.tsx` - `Accessor<number>` → plain `number` prop
   - `src/components/BottomHud.tsx` - `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()` hook
   - `src/components/CursorOverlay.tsx` - `onMount`/`onCleanup` → `useEffect` with cleanup
   - `src/components/HeroGallery.tsx` - `<For>` → `.map()`, `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()`

   **Phase 4 - Section Components (14 files):**
   All converted. Notable complex files:
   
   - `src/components/sections/ShowcaseSection.tsx` - Multiple exported components, `<For>` → `.map()`, classList → className, CSS custom properties cast as `React.CSSProperties`
   - `src/components/sections/BordersSection.tsx` - `<Show>` → ternary, nested `<For>` → `.map()`
   - `src/components/sections/FactionPreviewSection.tsx` - Complex: multiple `createEffect` → `useEffect` with deps, `Accessor<T>` props → plain `T`, `onInput` → `onChange`
   - `src/components/sections/ButtonsSection.tsx` - Accessor function items (`src: () => string`) → plain strings
   - `src/components/sections/BarsSection.tsx` - `Accessor<number>` → plain `number`
   - `src/components/sections/HeroSection.tsx` - Canvas ref callback → `React.RefObject`
   - `src/components/sections/DesignTokensSection.tsx` - Complex canvas rendering with ResizeObserver
   - `src/components/sections/BundleSizeSection.tsx` - `Accessor<string>` → plain `string`
   - `src/components/sections/FooterSection.tsx` - CSS custom property cast
   - `src/components/sections/PanelsSection.tsx` - `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()`

   **Phase 5 - WebGL Components (5 files):**
   
   - `src/components/TopHudWebGL.tsx` - All mutable GL state in `stateRef` useRef object, functions stored on state for cross-effect access, `propsRef` pattern for latest props in closures
   - `src/components/HeroPortraitModel.tsx` - Same stateRef pattern, complex async init with version tracking

input.prompt:
You need to convert `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/App.tsx` from SolidJS to React. This is the final phase (Phase 6) of a full SolidJS→React migration. All other files have already been converted.

Read the ENTIRE App.tsx file carefully (it's ~4360 lines), then rewrite it with React equivalents. Here are the conversion rules:

## Import changes
- Remove `import { createEffect, createSignal, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'`
- Add `import { useState, useEffect, useRef, useCallback, useMemo } from 'react'`
- `currentRace()` calls → `useCurrentRace()` hook. Import `useCurrentRace` from `'./state/race'` (it already exists)
- Keep `setCurrentRace` and `RACE_PREFIXES` imports from `'./state/race'`

## State changes
- `const [foo, setFoo] = createSignal(val)` → `const [foo, setFoo] = useState(val)`
- Access: `foo()` → `foo` (remove the function call parens)
- `let ref!: HTMLCanvasElement` → `const ref = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null)` and access via `ref.current`

## Effects
- `createEffect(() => { ... onCleanup(() => { cancelled = true; }); })` → `useEffect(() => { ... return () => { cancelled = true; }; }, [deps])`
- For effects that depend on `currentRace()`: use `useCurrentRace()` hook and put `race` in the dependency array
- For effects that depend on signal values: put those state values in the dependency array
- `onMount(() => {...})` → `useEffect(() => {...}, [])`
- `onCleanup(() => {...})` at component level → return cleanup from a `useEffect`

## JSX changes
- `class=` → `className=`
- `classList={{ 'foo': condition }}` → `className={`base${condition ? ' foo' : ''}`}` or combine into template string
- `foo()` in JSX → `foo` (plain state values)
- Signal accessor functions `() => string` in props → plain `string`
- Inline styles: keep as-is (React already uses objects), but convert kebab-case CSS properties to camelCase:
  - `'pointer-events'` → `pointerEvents`
  - `'background-image'` → `backgroundImage`  
  - `'font-size'` → `fontSize`
  - `'image-rendering'` → `imageRendering`
  - `'line-height'` → `lineHeight`
  - `'align-items'` → `alignItems`
  - etc.
- For `onInput` on inputs → `onChange`
- `ref={el}` where el is a `let` → `ref={refObj}` where refObj is `useRef`

## Derived values (computed from signals)
- `const foo = () => expr` that computes from signals → either inline the expression or use `useMemo`
- For simple derived values that are cheap to compute, just compute them inline during render (no need for useMemo)

## MenuFrameVariantCard component
- It's defined before App(). Convert it to React:
  - `let buttonCanvasRef!: HTMLCanvasElement` → `const buttonCanvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null)`
  - `createSignal` → `useState`
  - `createEffect` → `useEffect`
  - `onCleanup` inside effect → return cleanup
  - Props type: change `glueBgUrl: () => string` (accessor) → `glueBgUrl: string` (plain string)
  - In JSX: `props.glueBgUrl()` → `props.glueBgUrl`

## MenuFrameVariantCardProps interface
- Change all `() => string` accessor types to plain `string`

## Key patterns for the App component
The App component has:
1. ~30 canvas refs (let ref!: HTMLCanvasElement) → useRef
2. ~140 createSignal calls → useState
3. ~20 createEffect calls → useEffect with proper dependency arrays
4. Derived values (const foo = () => ...) → compute inline or useMemo
5. Timers (setInterval in onMount) → useEffect with cleanup
6. Viewport width listener → useEffect with cleanup

For the large effects that load many BLP textures based on `currentRace()`:
- Use `useCurrentRace()` at the top of App and use the value in useEffect deps

For effects that draw to canvases based on multiple signals:
- List all the signal values used inside the effect as dependencies

IMPORTANT: 
- `prev` parameters in setter callbacks need explicit types: `(prev: number) => ...`, `(prev: string) => ...`
- The `menuFrameVariants` computed value references many signal values — compute it during render (just evaluate it inline since it's an array)
- Do NOT change any of the pure functions outside the components (loadBlpDataUrl, drawGlueNineSliceToCanvas, etc.)
- Write the complete converted file using the Write tool

Read the entire file first, then write the converted version.

- `ref={el}` where el is a `let` → `ref={refObj}` where refObj is `useRef`

## Derived values (computed from signals)
- `const foo = () => expr` that computes from signals → either inline the expression or use `useMemo`

## MenuFrameVariantCardProps interface

Build succeeds. Phase 6 is complete — `App.tsx` has been fully converted from SolidJS to React.

Here's a summary of the full migration:

**Phase 6 — App.tsx conversion (completed by the agent):**
- `import { createEffect, createSignal, onCleanup, onMount } from 'solid-js'` → `import { useState, useEffect, useRef } from 'react'`
- `currentRace()` → `useCurrentRace()` hook
- ~30 `let ref!: HTMLCanvasElement` → `useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null)`
- ~140 `createSignal` → `useState`
- ~25 `createEffect`/`onMount`/`onCleanup` → `useEffect` with proper dependency arrays and cleanup returns
- All derived values (signal accessor functions) → inline computed values
- JSX: `class=` → `className=`, `classList` → template string className, kebab-case styles → camelCase
- Props: accessor types `() => string` → plain `string`

**Remaining TS errors (all pre-existing, not from migration):**
- Missing modules: `GlueButton`, `OptionButton`
- Missing exports: `HEROES`, `HeroData`, `TextureEntry`, `RACES`

The project builds successfully with `npm run build`.

content.text:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the comprehensive analysis.

## Project Library Consumption Analysis

Based on my thorough exploration, here's what I found:

### 1. **package.json** - Currently App-Only
- **Status**: `"private": true` - explicitly marked as private, not intended for npm publication
- **No Library Exports**: Missing all library-specific fields:
  - No `main` field
  - No `module` field
  - No `exports` field
  - No `files` field
  - No `types` field
- **Build Script**: Single generic `"build": "vite build"` with no library mode
- **Dependencies**: React 19.2.4, ReactDOM, gl-matrix, war3-model
- **Dev Stack**: TypeScript 5.7, Vite 6, React plugin

**File**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/package.json`

### 2. **vite.config.ts** - App Build Only
- **No Library Configuration**: No `build.lib` settings
- **App-Only Build**: Only specifies `target: 'esnext'` and React plugin
- **Output**: Produces a single HTML app bundle, not a reusable library

**File**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/vite.config.ts`

### 3. **tsconfig.json** - No Declaration Emission
- **No Type Generation**: Missing all declaration-related fields:
  - No `declaration: true`
  - No `emitDeclarationFiles`
  - No `outDir`
  - No `declarationDir`
- **noEmit: true**: Explicitly set to not emit anything (JSX check-only mode)
- **Target**: ESNext, moduleResolution: bundler

**File**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/tsconfig.json`

### 4. **src/index.tsx** - Application Entry Point
- Renders the React app to `#root` DOM element
- Imports `App` component, global styles, and tooltips CSS
- **Not a library entry point** - it's the web app bootstrap

**File**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/index.tsx`

### 5. **Barrel Files** - None Exist
- **No `src/index.ts` exports**
- **No `src/components/index.ts`** or any barrel files
- Components are only used internally via direct imports in the app

### 6. **Exported Components** - 17 UI Components (All Default Exports)
All components use default exports (no named exports):

**Primitive UI Components** (5):
- `StatBar` - Animated health/mana/XP bars with WC3 styling
- `Checkbox` - WC3-themed checkbox with WebGL decoration
- `Slider` - Draggable slider with custom WebGL rendering
- `RadioButton` - Radio button with WebGL checkbox styling
- `EditBox` - Text input field (imported in MenuButton)

**Complex Components** (7):
- `MenuButton` - Menu button with custom 9-slice border rendering
- `MenuPanel` - Menu container/panel wrapper
- `Tooltip` - Tooltip container with hover positioning
- `LoadingBar` - Animated progress bar
- `Checkbox`, `RadioButton` (listed above)

**Layout/HUD Components** (5):
- `TopHud` - Top console HUD with resources/time (requires Renderer)
- `TopHudWebGL` - Separate WebGL canvas for top HUD tiles
- `BottomHud` - Bottom HUD UI assembly
- `CursorOverlay` - Custom cursor rendering
- `HeroGallery` - Hero portrait selection gallery

**3D Model Components** (3):
- `HeroPortraitModel` - 3D MDX hero portrait (war3-model)
- `WorkerUnitModel` - 3D worker unit model (war3-model)
- `TimeIndicatorModel` - 3D clock/time display model

**File Locations**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/*.tsx`

### 7. **State Exports** - Modular but App-Centric

**`src/state/race.ts`**:
- `RACE_PREFIXES` - Race constant definitions
- `currentRace()` - Getter
- `setCurrentRace()` - Setter
- `useCurrentRace()` - React hook

**`src/state/resources.ts`**:
- `useAnimatedBars()` - Hook for health/mana/xp/build percentages
- `goldCurrent/Target`, `lumberCurrent/Target` - Getters
- `setGoldTarget`, `setLumberTarget` - Setters
- `useGoldCurrent/Target`, `useLumberCurrent/Target` - React hooks
- `tickResources()`, `animateCounters()` - Update functions
- `handleCheatKey()` - Cheat code processor

**`src/state/ui.ts`**:
- `useAnimatedBars()` - Custom hook (duplicate name!)
- `useLoadingBar()` - Loading bar animation hook

**`src/state/createStore.ts`**:
- `createExternalStore<T>()` - Reusable store factory (returns `{ get, set, useValue }`)

### 8. **Type Exports** - Minimal Public API

**`src/lib/types.ts`** (Complete File):
```typescript
export type Race = 'Human' | 'Orc' | 'NightElf' | 'Undead';
export interface RacePrefix {
  tile: string;
  esc: string;
  lower: string;
  display: string;
}
```

### 9. **Bundling Setup** - None Exists
- No Rollup config
- No tsup config
- No esbuild config
- Only Vite for app-mode builds

### 10. **Critical Architectural Issues for Library Use**

**Renderer Dependency**:
- Most components (Checkbox, Slider, StatBar, MenuButton, Tooltip, etc.) **require a `Renderer` instance prop**
- `Renderer` is a custom WebGL2 rendering engine in `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/renderer/Renderer.ts`
- Components register themselves with the renderer and it draws decorations each frame
- This **deeply couples components to the WebGL infrastructure**, making them unsuitable for standalone use

**No Component Re-export Structure**:
- No barrel files mean consumers would need to know exact file paths
- Components have no named exports (only defaults)

**CSS Dependencies**:
- `global.css` (~45KB) is required and imported at app bootstrap
- Components depend on BLP texture assets in `public/` directory
- No CSS extraction or scoped styling

**Directory Structure**:
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/` - 17 UI components
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/sections/` - 14 demo/showcase sections
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/renderer/` - WebGL engine
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/` - State management (4 files)
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/lib/` - Types (1 file)
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/data/` - Data definitions

---

## Summary: What Would Need to Change for `import { StatBar, Checkbox } from 'wcui'`

1. **Update `package.json`**:
   - Remove `"private": true`
   - Add `"main": "dist/index.js"`
   - Add `"module": "dist/index.es.js"`
   - Add `"types": "dist/index.d.ts"`
   - Add `"exports"` with conditional exports for ESM/CJS
   - Add `"files": ["dist"]`

2. **Create `src/index.ts`** - Barrel file re-exporting all public components and types:
   ```typescript
   // Components
   export { default as StatBar } from './components/StatBar';
   export { default as Checkbox } from './components/Checkbox';
   // ... (all 17 components)
   
   // State
   export { useCurrentRace, setCurrentRace, RACE_PREFIXES } from './state/race';
   export { useGoldCurrent, useGoldTarget } from './state/resources';
   export { createExternalStore } from './state/createStore';
   
   // Types
   export type { Race, RacePrefix } from './lib/types';
   ```

3. **Configure Vite for library build** - Update `vite.config.ts` with `build.lib`:
   ```typescript
   build: {
     lib: {
       entry: resolve(__dirname, 'src/index.ts'),
       name: 'WC3UI',
       formats: ['es', 'umd'],
       fileName: 'index',
     },
     rollupOptions: {
       external: ['react', 'react-dom'],
       output: { globals: { react: 'React' } },
     },
   }
   ```

4. **Enable TypeScript declarations** - Update `tsconfig.json`:
   - Set `"declaration": true`
   - Set `"declarationDir": "dist"`
   - Change `"noEmit": false`

5. **Address the Renderer dependency issue** (Major):
   - Either make Renderer optional with graceful fallback
   - Or export Renderer and document required setup
   - Or split into "core" (no Renderer) and "full" (with WebGL) packages

6. **Add `.npmignore` or files array** to exclude source/demo code

7. **Create setup guide** for consuming apps (CSS injection, asset paths, Renderer initialization)

8. **No existing bundling config** means using Vite's built-in library mode is the simplest path (no Rollup/tsup setup needed)

- `TopHudWebGL` - Separate WebGL canvas for top HUD tiles
- `BottomHud` - Bottom HUD UI assembly
- `CursorOverlay` - Custom cursor rendering
- `HeroGallery` - Hero portrait selection gallery

**3D Model Components** (3):
- `HeroPortraitModel` - 3D MDX hero portrait (war3-model)
- `WorkerUnitModel` - 3D worker unit model (war3-model)
- `TimeIndicatorModel` - 3D clock/time display model

**File Locations**: `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/*.tsx`

### 7. **State Exports** - Modular but App-Centric

**`src/state/race.ts`**:
- `RACE_PREFIXES` - Race constant definitions
- `currentRace()` - Getter
- `setCurrentRace()` - Setter
- `useCurrentRace()` - React hook

**`src/state/resources.ts`**:
- `useAnimatedBars()` - Hook for health/mana/xp/build percentages
- `goldCurrent/Target`, `lumberCurrent/Target` - Getters
- `setGoldTarget`, `setLumberTarget` - Setters
- `useGoldCurrent/Target`, `useLumberCurrent/Target` - React hooks
- `tickResources()`, `animateCounters()` - Update functions
- `handleCheatKey()` - Cheat code processor

**`src/state/ui.ts`**:
- `useAnimatedBars()` - Custom hook (duplicate name!)
- `useLoadingBar()` - Loading bar animation hook

**`src/state/createStore.ts`**:
- `createExternalStore<T>()` - Reusable store factory (returns `{ get, set, useValue }`)

### 8. **Type Exports** - Minimal Public API

**`src/lib/types.ts`** (Complete File):
```typescript
export type Race = 'Human' | 'Orc' | 'NightElf' | 'Undead';
export interface RacePrefix {
  tile: string;
  esc: string;
  lower: string;
  display: string;
}
```

### 9. **Bundling Setup** - None Exists
- No Rollup config
- No tsup config
- No esbuild config
- Only Vite for app-mode builds

### 10. **Critical Architectural Issues for Library Use**

**Renderer Dependency**:
- Most components (Checkbox, Slider, StatBar, MenuButton, Tooltip, etc.) **require a `Renderer` instance prop**
- `Renderer` is a custom WebGL2 rendering engine in `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/renderer/Renderer.ts`
- Components register themselves with the renderer and it draws decorations each frame
- This **deeply couples components to the WebGL infrastructure**, making them unsuitable for standalone use

**No Component Re-export Structure**:
- No barrel files mean consumers would need to know exact file paths
- Components have no named exports (only defaults)

**CSS Dependencies**:
- `global.css` (~45KB) is required and imported at app bootstrap
- Components depend on BLP texture assets in `public/` directory
- No CSS extraction or scoped styling

**Directory Structure**:
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/` - 17 UI components
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/components/sections/` - 14 demo/showcase sections
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/renderer/` - WebGL engine
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/` - State management (4 files)
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/lib/` - Types (1 file)
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/data/` - Data definitions

---

## Summary: What Would Need to Change for `import { StatBar, Checkbox } from 'wcui'`

1. **Update `package.json`**:
   - Remove `"private": true`
   - Add `"main": "dist/index.js"`
   - Add `"module": "dist/index.es.js"`
   - Add `"types": "dist/index.d.ts"`
   - Add `"exports"` with conditional exports for ESM/CJS
   - Add `"files": ["dist"]`

2. **Create `src/index.ts`** - Barrel file re-exporting all public components and types:
   ```typescript
   // Components
   export { default as StatBar } from './components/StatBar';
   export { default as Checkbox } from './components/Checkbox';
   // ... (all 17 components)
   
   // State
   export { useCurrentRace, setCurrentRace, RACE_PREFIXES } from './state/race';
   export { useGoldCurrent, useGoldTarget } from './state/resources';
   export { createExternalStore } from './state/createStore';
   
   // Types
   export type { Race, RacePrefix } from './lib/types';
   ```

3. **Configure Vite for library build** - Update `vite.config.ts` with `build.lib`:
   ```typescript
   build: {
     lib: {
       entry: resolve(__dirname, 'src/index.ts'),
       name: 'WC3UI',
       formats: ['es', 'umd'],
       fileName: 'index',
     },
     rollupOptions: {
       external: ['react', 'react-dom'],
       output: { globals: { react: 'React' } },
     },
   }
   ```

4. **Enable TypeScript declarations** - Update `tsconfig.json`:
   - Set `"declaration": true`
   - Set `"declarationDir": "dist"`
   - Change `"noEmit": false`

5. **Address the Renderer dependency issue** (Major):
   - Either make Renderer optional with graceful fallback
   - Or export Renderer and document required setup
   - Or split into "core" (no Renderer) and "full" (with WebGL) packages

6. **Add `.npmignore` or files array** to exclude source/demo code

7. **Create setup guide** for consuming apps (CSS injection, asset paths, Renderer initialization)

8. **No existing bundling config** means using Vite's built-in library mode is the simplest path (no Rollup/tsup setup needed)

The exploration reveals that this project is currently a **standalone demo app**, not a consumable library. There's no library build, no barrel exports, no type declarations — just a Vite app entry point.

Let me design the plan.

Right now this project **cannot** be imported by another React project — it's structured purely as a standalone app. To make it work, several things need to change. Before I plan the specifics, I need to understand your intent:

questions.question: How do you want to consume this library in the other project?

options.label: npm link / local path
options.description: Install from local filesystem via `npm link` or `"wcui": "file:../wcui"` in package.json. No npm publish needed.

options.label: Publish to npm
options.description: Full npm package setup with proper package.json fields, so you can `npm install wcui` from a registry.

options.label: Git dependency
options.description: Install directly from the git repo via `"wcui": "github:user/wcui"`. Requires a build step but no registry.

questions.question: What do you want to export from the library? The components are tightly coupled to a WebGL Renderer, ~45KB of global CSS, and BLP texture assets in public/.

options.label: Everything as-is
options.description: Export all components, state, renderer, and types. The consuming app must set up the Renderer, load the CSS, and serve the BLP assets from public/.

options.label: Components + state only
options.description: Export components and state hooks. Keep renderer internal. Consuming app still needs the CSS and assets.

options.label: Just make it importable
options.description: Minimal setup: barrel exports, library build config, and type declarations. Don't restructure anything — just wire up the packaging so imports work.

I need to plan converting a React + Vite demo app into a publishable npm package. Here's the context:

**Current state:**
- Project at `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui` — a WC3 UI component library demo app
- Built with React 19 + TypeScript + Vite 6
- `package.json` has `"private": true`, no `main`/`module`/`types`/`exports`/`files` fields
- `vite.config.ts` has no `build.lib` config — just app build
- `tsconfig.json` has `"noEmit": true`, no `declaration` settings
- `src/index.tsx` is the app entry point (renders `<App />` to DOM)
- No barrel export files exist anywhere
- Components are in `src/components/` (17 files, all default exports)
- State hooks in `src/state/` (race.ts, resources.ts, ui.ts, createStore.ts)
- Types in `src/lib/types.ts` (Race, RacePrefix)
- WebGL renderer in `src/renderer/` (Renderer.ts, NineSlice.ts, QuadBatcher.ts, TextureManager.ts, textures.ts)
- ~45KB global CSS in `src/global.css` + `src/showcase-tooltip-center.css`
- BLP texture assets in `public/` — these must NOT be included in the package (copyright). Consumer provides them.

**Requirements:**
1. Publish as npm package so other React projects can `import { StatBar, Checkbox, ... } from 'wcui'`
2. Exclude `public/` assets from the package — consuming projects will serve these from their own public/
3. Components reference assets via absolute paths like `/console-buttons/...`, `/buttons/...` — this works as long as consumer serves files at those paths
4. Export components, state hooks, types, and the Renderer (needed by several components)
5. Keep the demo app working too (both `npm run dev` for the demo and `npm run build:lib` for the library)

**Key files to read:**
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/package.json`
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/vite.config.ts`
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/tsconfig.json`

- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/ui.ts`
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/state/createStore.ts`
- `/home/garrosh/repos/wcui/src/renderer/Renderer.ts` (just the exports)
- List files in `src/components/` and `src/components/sections/`

**Design the plan covering:**
1. What new files to create (barrel export file)
2. What changes to package.json (fields to add, scripts)
3. What changes to vite.config.ts (library build mode with rollup externals)
4. What changes to tsconfig.json (declaration generation) — consider whether to use a separate tsconfig.build.json
5. Whether to use Vite's built-in library mode or a separate tool like tsup
6. How CSS should be handled (Vite library mode extracts CSS to a separate file consumers import)
7. How to keep both the demo app build and library build working
8. What the consumer's usage would look like

Please read the key files and provide a detailed implementation plan.

input.content:
# Plan: Package wcui as an npm library

## Context
The project is currently a standalone Vite demo app. The goal is to make it publishable as an npm package so its React components can be imported in other projects. Assets in `public/` (BLP textures) are excluded due to Blizzard copyright — consuming projects must serve those files themselves.

## Steps

### 1. Create `src/lib.ts` — library barrel export
New file that re-exports all public components, state, renderer, and types.

- Components from `src/components/`: `StatBar`, `Checkbox`, `Slider`, `RadioButton`, `EditBox`, `MenuButton`, `MenuPanel`, `Tooltip`, `LoadingBar`, `TopHud`, `TopHudWebGL`, `BottomHud`, `HeroPortraitModel`, `WorkerUnitModel`, `TimeIndicatorModel`, `CursorOverlay`, `HeroGallery`
- State from `src/state/`: `createExternalStore`, `useCurrentRace`, `setCurrentRace`, `RACE_PREFIXES`, resource hooks/getters/setters, `useAnimatedBars`, `useLoadingBar`
- Renderer: `Renderer` class + all 13 registration interfaces from `src/renderer/Renderer.ts`
- Types: `Race`, `RacePrefix` from `src/lib/types.ts`
- Import `./global.css` so Vite extracts it as `dist/style.css`
- Do NOT export section components (`src/components/sections/`) — those are demo-only

### 2. Create `vite.lib.config.ts` — library build config
Separate Vite config (keeps demo config untouched):

- `build.lib.entry`: `src/lib.ts`
- `build.lib.formats`: `['es', 'cjs']`
- `build.lib.fileName`: `wcui.js` / `wcui.cjs`
- `rollupOptions.external`: `['react', 'react-dom', 'react/jsx-runtime', 'react-dom/client']`
- `build.cssFileName`: `'style.css'`
- `build.sourcemap`: `true`

### 3. Create `tsconfig.build.json` — declaration generation
Extends `tsconfig.json` with overrides:

- `noEmit: false`, `declaration: true`, `emitDeclarationOnly: true`
- `declarationDir: "dist"`
- `types: []` (exclude `vite/client` from declarations)
- `include`: `src/lib.ts` + component/state/renderer/lib directories

### 4. Modify `package.json`
- `"name": "wcui"`, `"private": false`, `"version": "0.1.0"`
- Add `"main"`, `"module"`, `"types"`, `"exports"`, `"files": ["dist"]`, `"sideEffects": ["*.css"]`
- Move `react`/`react-dom` from `dependencies` → `peerDependencies` (keep in `devDependencies` for demo)
- Keep `gl-matrix` and `war3-model` in `dependencies` (bundled into library output)
- Add script `"build:lib": "vite build --config vite.lib.config.ts && tsc -p tsconfig.build.json"`
- Add `"prepublishOnly": "npm run build:lib"`

### 5. Verify

1. `npm run build:lib` — check `dist/` contains `wcui.js`, `wcui.cjs`, `style.css`, `lib.d.ts` + other `.d.ts` files
2. `npm run dev` — demo app still works
3. `npm pack` — inspect tarball contains only `dist/` + metadata (no `public/` assets)
4. Test in a consumer project: `npm install ../wcui/wcui-0.1.0.tgz`, import a component, verify it renders

## Consumer usage
```tsx
// main.tsx
import 'wcui/style.css';

// any component
import { StatBar, Checkbox, useCurrentRace } from 'wcui';
import type { Race } from 'wcui';
```
Consumer must serve WC3 BLP/PNG assets from their own `public/` at the same paths the components expect (e.g., `/console-buttons/...`, `/buttons/...`, `/bars/...`).

## Files modified
- `package.json` — add library fields, restructure deps
- New: `src/lib.ts`
- New: `vite.lib.config.ts`
- New: `tsconfig.build.json`

content.text: The renderer is still being pulled in because components do `import type { Renderer }` which TypeScript follows. The `exclude` doesn't prevent transitive includes. Let me use a different approach — only include the specific directories needed and explicitly exclude renderer:

input.content:

  "extends": "./tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "noEmit": false,
    "declaration": true,
    "emitDeclarationOnly": true,
    "declarationDir": "dist",
    "outDir": "dist",
    "types": [],
    "skipLibCheck": true
  },
  "include": [
    "src/lib.ts",
    "src/lib",
    "src/state",
    "src/components/*.tsx",
    "src/renderer"
  ],
  "exclude": [
    "src/components/MenuButton.tsx",
    "src/components/HeroGallery.tsx",
    "src/renderer/textures.ts"
  ]
}

content.text:
Everything looks correct. The package contains:
- `dist/wcui.js` + `.cjs` (bundles)
- `dist/style.css` (extracted CSS)
- `dist/lib.d.ts` + all component/state/renderer `.d.ts` files
- `package.json`
- No `public/` assets, no source files

Here's a summary of what was done:

**New files created:**
- `src/lib.ts` — barrel export for all components, state, renderer, and types
- `vite.lib.config.ts` — Vite library build config (externalizes React, disables public dir copy)
- `tsconfig.build.json` — declaration-only TypeScript config for `.d.ts` generation

**Files modified:**
- `package.json` — added `exports`, `main`, `module`, `types`, `files`, `sideEffects`, `peerDependencies`, `build:lib` script; moved react to peer deps
- `src/lib/types.ts` — added missing `TextureEntry` interface (was referenced but never defined)
- `src/state/race.ts` — added missing `RACES` array export (was referenced but never defined)

**Excluded from library** (pre-existing broken imports):
- `MenuButton` (depends on non-existent `GlueButton`)
- `HeroGallery` (depends on non-existent `HEROES`/`HeroData`)

**Consumer usage:**
```tsx
// Install: npm install wcui
// Then in your app:
import 'wcui/style.css';                              // once at app root
import { StatBar, Checkbox, useCurrentRace } from 'wcui';
import type { Race } from 'wcui';
```
The consumer must serve the WC3 assets from their own `public/` directory at the same paths (e.g., `/buttons/...`, `/bars/...`).

content.text: This is a straightforward guidance task — no new code changes needed. The library is already built and packaged correctly. The plan just covers the three local install methods and their trade-offs.

input.content:
# Plan: Local library consumption (no npm publish)

## Context
The wcui library build is already set up (`npm run build:lib` → `dist/`). The user wants to use it in another React project without publishing to a registry. This plan covers the three local install methods and which to use.

## No code changes needed
The library packaging is already complete. This is purely a usage guide.

---

## Method 1: `file:` path (Recommended for ongoing development)

In the **consumer project's** `package.json`:
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "wcui": "file:../wcui"
  }
}
```
Then run `npm install` in the consumer project.

**How it works:** npm copies the `dist/` folder (controlled by `"files": ["dist"]` in wcui's package.json) into `node_modules/wcui`. Re-run `npm install` in the consumer after each `npm run build:lib` in wcui.

**Pros:** Simple. Works without any extra steps beyond building.
**Cons:** Must manually rebuild + reinstall after changes.

---

## Method 2: `npm pack` tarball (Best for one-time testing)

```bash
# In wcui/
npm run build:lib
npm pack
# Creates wcui-0.1.0.tgz

# In consumer project/
npm install ../wcui/wcui-0.1.0.tgz
```

**Pros:** Exactly simulates what a real `npm install wcui` would do. Safe, no symlinks.
**Cons:** Must re-pack and re-install after every change.

---

## Method 3: `npm link` (Best for active co-development with live symlink)

```bash
# In wcui/
npm run build:lib
npm link

# In consumer project/
npm link wcui
```

After any change to wcui, just run `npm run build:lib` — the consumer immediately sees updated `dist/` via symlink without reinstalling.

**Pros:** No reinstall needed after rebuilds. Fastest iteration.
**Cons:** Symlinks can cause React "multiple instances" errors if both projects have their own `node_modules/react`. Fix with:
```bash
# In wcui/, point its react to the consumer's copy:
npm link ../my-consumer-project/node_modules/react
```

---

## Consumer setup (all methods)

After installing, in the consumer project:

```tsx
// src/main.tsx (app entry)
import 'wcui/style.css';

// any component file
import { StatBar, Checkbox, useCurrentRace, setCurrentRace } from 'wcui';
import type { Race } from 'wcui';
```

The consumer must also serve WC3 assets from their `public/` at the expected paths (`/buttons/`, `/bars/`, `/console-buttons/`, etc.).

---

## Recommendation
Use **Method 1 (`file:`)** to start — it's the simplest. Switch to **Method 3 (`npm link`)** if you're actively developing both projects simultaneously.

## Files involved
- `wcui/package.json` — already configured with `"files": ["dist"]` and correct `exports`
- `wcui/dist/` — rebuilt via `npm run build:lib` before any install

## Files involved
- `wcui/package.json` — already configured with `"files": ["dist"]` and correct `exports`
- `wcui/dist/` — rebuilt via `npm run build:lib` before any install

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Plan: Package wcui as an npm library

## Context
The project is currently a standalone Vite demo app. The goal is to make it publishable as an npm package so its React components can be imported in other projects. Assets in `public/` (BLP textures) are excluded due to Blizzard copyright — consuming projects must serve those files themselves.

## Steps

### 1. Create `src/lib.ts` — library barrel export
New file that re-exports all public components, state, renderer, and types.

- Components from `src/components/`: `StatBar`, `Checkbox`, `Slider`, `RadioButton`, `EditBox`, `MenuButton`, `MenuPanel`, `Tooltip`, `LoadingBar`, `TopHud`, `TopHudWebGL`, `BottomHud`, `HeroPortraitModel`, `WorkerUnitModel`, `TimeIndicatorModel`, `CursorOverlay`, `HeroGallery`
- State from `src/state/`: `createExternalStore`, `useCurrentRace`, `setCurrentRace`, `RACE_PREFIXES`, resource hooks/getters/setters, `useAnimatedBars`, `useLoadingBar`
- Renderer: `Renderer` class + all 13 registration interfaces from `src/renderer/Renderer.ts`
- Types: `Race`, `RacePrefix` from `src/lib/types.ts`
- Import `./global.css` so Vite extracts it as `dist/style.css`
- Do NOT export section components (`src/components/sections/`) — those are demo-only

### 2. Create `vite.lib.config.ts` — library build config
Separate Vite config (keeps demo config untouched):

- `build.lib.entry`: `src/lib.ts`
- `build.lib.formats`: `['es', 'cjs']`
- `build.lib.fileName`: `wcui.js` / `wcui.cjs`
- `rollupOptions.external`: `['react', 'react-dom', 'react/jsx-runtime', 'react-dom/client']`
- `build.cssFileName`: `'style.css'`
- `build.sourcemap`: `true`

### 3. Create `tsconfig.build.json` — declaration generation
Extends `tsconfig.json` with overrides:

- `noEmit: false`, `declaration: true`, `emitDeclarationOnly: true`
- `declarationDir: "dist"`
- `types: []` (exclude `vite/client` from declarations)
- `include`: `src/lib.ts` + component/state/renderer/lib directories

### 4. Modify `package.json`
- `"name": "wcui"`, `"private": false`, `"version": "0.1.0"`
- Add `"main"`, `"module"`, `"types"`, `"exports"`, `"files": ["dist"]`, `"sideEffects": ["*.css"]`
- Move `react`/`react-dom` from `dependencies` → `peerDependencies` (keep in `devDependencies` for demo)
- Keep `gl-matrix` and `war3-model` in `dependencies` (bundled into library output)
- Add script `"build:lib": "vite build --config vite.lib.config.ts && tsc -p tsconfig.build.json"`
- Add `"prepublishOnly": "npm run build:lib"`

### 5. Verify

1. `npm run build:lib` — check `dist/` contains `wcui.js`, `wcui.cjs`, `style.css`, `lib.d.ts` + other `.d.ts` files
2. `npm run dev` — demo app still works
3. `npm pack` — inspect tarball contains only `dist/` + metadata (no `public/` assets)
4. Test in a consumer project: `npm install ../wcui/wcui-0.1.0.tgz`, import a component, verify it renders

## Consumer usage
```tsx
// main.tsx
import 'wcui/style.css';

// any component
import { StatBar, Checkbox, useCurrentRace } from 'wcui';
import type { Race } from 'wcui';
```
Consumer must serve WC3 BLP/PNG assets from their own `public/` at the same paths the components expect (e.g., `/console-buttons/...`, `/buttons/...`, `/bars/...`).

## Files modified
- `package.json` — add library fields, restructure deps
- New: `src/lib.ts`
- New: `vite.lib.config.ts`
- New: `tsconfig.build.json`

# Plan: Local library consumption (no npm publish)

## Context
The wcui library build is already set up (`npm run build:lib` → `dist/`). The user wants to use it in another React project without publishing to a registry. This plan covers the three local install methods and which to use.

## No code changes needed
The library packaging is already complete. This is purely a usage guide.

---

## Method 1: `file:` path (Recommended for ongoing development)

In the **consumer project's** `package.json`:
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "wcui": "file:../wcui"
  }
}
```
Then run `npm install` in the consumer project.

**How it works:** npm copies the `dist/` folder (controlled by `"files": ["dist"]` in wcui's package.json) into `node_modules/wcui`. Re-run `npm install` in the consumer after each `npm run build:lib` in wcui.

**Pros:** Simple. Works without any extra steps beyond building.
**Cons:** Must manually rebuild + reinstall after changes.

---

## Method 2: `npm pack` tarball (Best for one-time testing)

```bash
# In wcui/
npm run build:lib
npm pack
# Creates wcui-0.1.0.tgz

# In consumer project/
npm install ../wcui/wcui-0.1.0.tgz
```

**Pros:** Exactly simulates what a real `npm install wcui` would do. Safe, no symlinks.
**Cons:** Must re-pack and re-install after every change.

---

## Method 3: `npm link` (Best for active co-development with live symlink)

```bash
# In wcui/
npm run build:lib
npm link

# In consumer project/
npm link wcui
```

After any change to wcui, just run `npm run build:lib` — the consumer immediately sees updated `dist/` via symlink without reinstalling.

**Pros:** No reinstall needed after rebuilds. Fastest iteration.
**Cons:** Symlinks can cause React "multiple instances" errors if both projects have their own `node_modules/react`. Fix with:
```bash
# In wcui/, point its react to the consumer's copy:
npm link ../my-consumer-project/node_modules/react
```

---

## Consumer setup (all methods)

After installing, in the consumer project:

```tsx
// src/main.tsx (app entry)
import 'wcui/style.css';

// any component file
import { StatBar, Checkbox, useCurrentRace, setCurrentRace } from 'wcui';
import type { Race } from 'wcui';
```

The consumer must also serve WC3 assets from their `public/` at the expected paths (`/buttons/`, `/bars/`, `/console-buttons/`, etc.).

---

## Recommendation
Use **Method 1 (`file:`)** to start — it's the simplest. Switch to **Method 3 (`npm link`)** if you're actively developing both projects simultaneously.

## Files involved
- `wcui/package.json` — already configured with `"files": ["dist"]` and correct `exports`
- `wcui/dist/` — rebuilt via `npm run build:lib` before any install
